target.name: Tempo
target.founder: Nima Rahmatian, Kelvin Randhir, Joerg Petrasch, Alessandro Bo

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Tempo is a Series A-stage company in the Climate & Energy sector founded by Nima Rahmatian, Kelvin Randhir, Joerg Petrasch, and Alessandro Bo. The valuation framework draws from a comparable universe of 3 private peers at the same stage and sector (Graphyte, Fourth Power, and Steady Energy), with broader reference to 8 total private companies and 1 public comparable in the climate tech space.

Two valuation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach uses the $50M in total capital raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A progression, implying a $100M valuation. The peer-median method anchors to the median valuation of the three same-sector, same-stage comparables, which stands at $150M, with a range spanning $110M (Steady Energy) to $200M (Graphyte).

Triangulating these methods yields a valuation range of $85M to $172M, with a median of $125M. The $40M spread between methods reflects variance in peer valuations and the inherent sensitivity of stage-progression multiples to baseline assumptions. Key uncertainties include whether the $50M raised proxy accurately reflects fair value entry, the applicability of the peer set given potential differences in technology maturity and capital efficiency, and whether market conditions at the time of these comparables remain current.
